coredata.dc:title: A novel electrochemiluminescent immunosensor based on the quenching effect of aminated graphene on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Jing
Han, Tongqian
Ma, Hongmin
Yan, Tao
Pang, Xuehui
Li, Yueyun
Wei, Qin
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with an average diameter of 2 nm were synthesized by carbonization of diethylene triamine pentacetate acid (DTPA). The simple prepared N-CQDs showed excellent electrochemiluminescence (ECL) property and were used as luminophors to fabricate a sandwich-type ECL immunosensor. Aminated graphene (NH2-G) was also synthesized and used as a label of secondary antibody. The labeled NH2-G could effectively quench the ECL of N-CQDs modified on electrodes due to ECL resonance energy transfer (ERET). Immunological recognition which induced ECL quenching enabled the quantitative determination of biomarkers. Alpha fetoprotein (AFP) was selected as a model analyte to investigate the analytical performance of the proposed immunosensor. Under optimal conditions, a good linear relationship between ECL intensity and the logarithm of AFP concentration was obtained in the range of 0.01–100 ng mL−1 with the detection limit of 3.3 pg mL−1. The proposed ECL immunosensor showed good stability, acceptable selectivity and reproducibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Resonance energy transfer
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Alpha fetoprotein
Aminated graphene

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the sandwich-type ECL immunosensor
ECL quenching mechanism between NH2-G and N-CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A) and FT-IR spectroscopy of N-CQDs (B).
The effect of pH (A) and the concentration of K2S2O8 (B) on the response of ECL intensity from N-CQDs. Error bar = RSD (n = 5).

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of 2.5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4−and 0.1 M KCl at (a) bare GCE, (b) GCE/N-CQDs, (c) GCE/N-CQDs/Ab1, (d) GCE/N-CQDs/Ab1/BSA (e) GCE/N-CQDs/Ab1/BSA/AFP and (f)GCE/N-CQDs/Ab1/BSA/AFP/NH2-G@Ab2.
The ECL quenching mechanism between NH2-G and the N-CQDs.
Calibration curve of the immunosensor for different concentrations of AFP. Error bar = RSD (n = 5).

(A) Stability of ECL emissions from GCE/N-CQDs/Ab1/BSA under continuous scanning for 8 cycles in PBS (pH = 7.5) containing 0.1 M KCl and 20 mM K2S2O8. (B) The column of interference (1) 100 ng/mL glucose, (2) 100 ng/mL CEA, (3) 100 ng/mL CA15-3, (4) 1 ng/mL AFP, (5) 1 ng/mL AFP+100 ng/mL glucose, (6) 1 ng/mL AFP+100 ng/mL CEA, (7) 1 ng/mL AFP+100 ng/mL CA15-3 (C) Reproducibility of ECL emission. Error bar = RSD (n = 5).
Schematic illustration of the fabrication procedure of the immunosensor.
Comparison of different methods for the detection of AFP.
Determination of AFP in human serum sample.

head.ce:title: A novel electrochemiluminescent immunosensor based on the quenching effect of aminated graphene on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Sensing & Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Sensing & Analysis in Universities of Shandong
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255049, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with an average diameter of 2 nm were synthesized by carbonization of diethylene triamine pentacetate acid (DTPA). The simple prepared N-CQDs showed excellent electrochemiluminescence (ECL) property and were used as luminophors to fabricate a sandwich-type ECL immunosensor. Aminated graphene (NH2-G) was also synthesized and used as a label of secondary antibody. The labeled NH2-G could effectively quench the ECL of N-CQDs modified on electrodes due to ECL resonance energy transfer (ERET). Immunological recognition which induced ECL quenching enabled the quantitative determination of biomarkers. Alpha fetoprotein (AFP) was selected as a model analyte to investigate the analytical performance of the proposed immunosensor. Under optimal conditions, a good linear relationship between ECL intensity and the logarithm of AFP concentration was obtained in the range of 0.01–100 ng mL−1 with the detection limit of 3.3 pg mL−1. The proposed ECL immunosensor showed good stability, acceptable selectivity and reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received increasing attention because they show some advantages over the traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), such as high aqueous solubility, good chemical stability, high resistance to photobleaching, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [1]. Therefore, CQDs have been widely used in the fields of bioimaging, biological labeling and drug delivery [2,3]. Recently, many methods have been reported for synthesis of CQDs, such as electrochemical oxidation [4], hydrothermal methods [5] and acidic oxidation [6]. However, most of these methods need multi-step operations and strong acids as well as post-treatments with surface passivated agents to improve water solubility and fluorescent luminescence properties [7,8], which is complicated to fabricate electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor. To overcome these disadvantages, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized by carbonization of diethylene triamine pentacetate acid (DTPA) without use any passivated agents.

Due to the high sensitivity, well controllability and low background [9], ECL has gradually become an important and powerful analytical tool in many fields, including pharmaceutical analysis [10], immunoassay [11] and environmental pollution determination [8]. ECL signals could be obtained from the excited states of an ECL luminophore generated at the electrode surfaces back to ground states during the electrochemical reaction. Ru(bpy)3 2+ [12,13], luminol [14] and various quantum dots [15] have been used as traditional ECL luminophors. Among them, quantum dots, especially CQDs, have been attracted considerable attention due to its unique advantages. Furthermore, the ECL behavior of water-soluble CQDs has been reported and the luminescence mechanism has been proposed [16]. This work potentially opens up a promising avenue for the use of CQDs as an ECL reagent. As the extension of carbon nanomaterials, N-CQDs has been further explored in ECL field for the detection of metal ions, such as Fe3+ [17] and Hg2+ [8]. Despite these contributions, the exploitation of N-CQDs in ECL immunosensor is less well developed. This phenomena might be caused by the relative low ECL intensity after further surface modification. For applying N-CQDs to fabricate immunosensor, not only the synthetic method of N-CQDs was improved, but also the quenching effect of aminated graphene (NH2-G) on N-CQDs ECL was investigated to improve the detection sensitivity.

NH2-G was commonly used in the fabrication of immunosensor for its abundant primary amine groups. These ionizable groups can not only improve the solubility of graphene, but also facilitate the graphene assembled or reacted with other polymers, organic molecules and biological systems [18]. Furthermore, large amounts of secondary antibodies can be immobilized on the NH2-G due to its large specific surface area and good biocompatibility. In addition, graphene oxide and the reduced graphene have been well recognized as quenching nanomaterials to a variety of luminophors, so the quenching effect of NH2-G on the ECL of N-CQDs was investigated in this work.

Using N-CQDs as luminophors and NH2-G as quenching labels of secondary antibody, a sandwich-type ECL immunosensor was designed. The immunological recognition on electrode surface induced the quenching of N-CQDs ECL due to ECL resonance energy transfer (ERET), which enabled the determination of AFP antigens. As a member of the albuminoid gene family, AFP is an oncogenic glycoprotein which is normally expressed during gestation and originally identified in the human fetus in 1956 [19,20]. Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is the ﬁfth common cancer in the world, and AFP was one of the most important markers in the diagnosis and targeting of HCC [21]. Tumor marker analysis found a signiﬁcantly elevated AFP of 135,070 IU/mL (normal, <7) [22]. The proposed ECL immunosensor showed good stability, acceptable selectivity and reproducibility.

All reagents of analytical reagent grade were used directly for the following experiments without further purification. Human AFP antibody (Ab) and AFP were purchased from Shanghai Linc-Bio Science Co., Ltd., China. 1-(3-(Dimethyla-mino)-propyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98.5%), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%), bovine serum albumin (BSA) (96%–99%), diethylene triamine pentacetate acid (DTPA) and chitosan (CHIT) were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich (Beijing, China). All the electrochemical measurements were conducted in phosphate buffered saline (PBS containing 0.1 M KCl) as an electrolyte. Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ cm, 24 °C) was used for all of the experiments.

The ECL experiments were carried out on a MPI-F flow-injection chemiluminescence detector (Xi'an remax Electronic Science Tech. Co. Ltd., China) and electrochemical measurements were carried out on CHI760D electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instrument Shanghai Co., Ltd., China) using a three-electrode system consisted of a platinum wire as an auxiliary electrode, an Ag/AgCl electrode as reference electrode, and a glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 4 mm in diameter) as working electrode. Elemental analysis was recorded by a JEOL JSM-6700F microscope (Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were collected on an LS-45/55 PL spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, America). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained from an H-800 microscope (Hitachi, Japan). XRD patterns of the prepared samples were acquired with a Rigaku D/MAX 2200 X-ray diffractometer (Tokyo, Japan) using CuKα radiation (40 kV, 300 mA) of wavelength 0.154 nm to confirm the structure of the materials (Bragg equation 2d sinθ = nλ, n = 1, λ = 0.154 nm). The UV–vis absorbance spectra were examined with a Lambda 25 UV–vis Spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, American).

The N-CQDs were synthesized by the carbonization of DTPA according to our previous reports [23]. In brief, DTPA (2 g) was dispersed into ultrapure water (20 mL) under stirring. Subsequently, the solution turned colorless with heating. The heating process (<250 °C) was continued until the colorless solution became yellow clustered solid, indicating the formation of N-CQDs. Then, the obtained raw products were dissolved into ultrapure water (20 mL). After fully dissolved, the yellow crude N-CQDs solution was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 20 min to remove the excess DTPA. At last, the obtained solution was freeze-dried to get pure N-CQDs. N-CQDs (40 mg) were dissolved into CHIT solution (4 mL, 0.5%) with ultrasonication to prepare N-CQDs solution at concentration of 10 mg mL−1 and stored at 4 °C for further use.

Graphene oxide was prepared from natural graphite powder by a modified Hummer's method [24]. Graphene power (0.3 g), KMnO4 (1.8 g), and 36 mL of 9:1 mixture of concentrated H2SO4/H3PO4 were added into a 100 mL three-necked, round-bottomed flask and reacted at the temperature of 50 °C under stirring for 12 h. Then the products were poured onto 40 mL of ice, with 30% H2O2 (500 μL) under stirring. Subsequently, the products were collected by centrifugation (30 min, 8000 rpm) and washed with 0.2 M HCl, ethyl alcohol and aether respectively. Finally, the products were vacuum-dried overnight at 35 °C.

NH2-G was prepared according to the method previously reported [25]. 200 mg of prepared GO was added into 80 mL of ethylene glycol under ultrasonication, followed by the addition of ammonia water (2 mL). Then, the dark brown solution was transferred into Teflon lined autoclave for solvothermal reaction at 180 °C for 10 h. The products were collected by filtration and washed several times with ultrapure water. After that, the products were dried at 60 °C for 24 h.

To generate the NH2-G@Ab2, NH2-G solution (1 mL, 2 mg/mL) was mixed with Ab2 (l mL, 10 μg/mL) and oscillated for 12 h at the temperature of 4 °C. Subsequently, the uncombined Ab2 was removed by centrifugation and the NH2-G@Ab2 was dispersed into 1 mL of pH 7.5 PBS and stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the sandwich-type ECL immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The whole process for constructing the immunosensor was illustrated in Scheme 1 . (i) GCE electrode was polished carefully with 1.0, 0.3 and 0.05 μm alumina powder and then thoroughly washed with ultrapure water. (ii) A 6 μL of the N-CQDs was deposited on the electrode surface. After being almost dried under ambient conditions, 3 μL of the EDC/NHS was dropped and reacted for 30 min. After washed, 6 μL of Ab1 (1 μg/mL, in pH 7.5 PBS) was dropped on the electrode surface and incubated at the room temperature for 30 min. Then, 6 μL of 1% BSA solution was dropped and incubated for 30 min to block nonspecific binding, followed by washing and dropping 6 μL of AFP on the electrode surface. (iii) Finally, the immunosensor was further incubated with 3 μL of the prepared NH2-G@Ab2 for 30 min at the room temperature and then rinsed thoroughly with water to remove the unbound secondary antibodies.

This experiment was conducted in 10 mL of pH 7.5 PBS containing 100 mM KCl and 80 mM K2S2O8. The scanning potential range was −2.0 ∼ 0 V, the photomultiplier tube (PMT) was set at 650 V and scanning rate was set at 100 mV/s. Then, the modified electrode was placed in the ECL cell to measure the ECL signals.

The morphology of the N-CQDs was characterized by TEM. Fig. 1 A showed the TEM image of the N-CQDs. It can be seen that N-CQDs are mostly spherical dots with an average diameter of 2 nm. Fig. 1B showed the FT-IR spectrum of N-CQDs in the wavelength range of 750–4000 cm−1. The synthesized N-CQDs showed main absorption bands related to carboxyl from the O–H stretching vibration at 3430 cm−1, the CO stretching vibration at 1734 cm−1 and the C–O bending vibration at 1385 cm−1. The bands at 2800-3000 cm−1 were attributed to the C–H bond stretching vibrations. The characteristic stretch of the non-conjugated C–N was observed at 1221 cm−1. These functional groups improved the hydrophilicity and stability of N-CQDs in aqueous systems. Fig. S2 (See supporting information Fig. S2) showed the FT-IR spectrum of NH2-G in the wavelength range of 1000–4000 cm−1. In the curve of NH2-G, the peak of 3427 cm−1 and 2306 cm−1 were attributed to stretching vibrations of N–H. The band at 1582 cm−1 was the characteristic stretching vibrations of CC. The peak of 1177 cm−1 was corresponding to the C–N stretching vibrations. Fig. S3A showed the SEM image of NH2-G. NH2-G is rippled and it resembles waves of crumpled silk veils (See supporting information Fig. S3). The TEM image (Fig. S3B) showed wrinkled paper-like structure of large NH2-G. The UV–vis spectrums (Fig. S1A, curve a) were used to further confirm the successful formation of N-CQDs. As the figure showed, the UV–vis spectrum of N-CQDs exhibited a typical absorption at 365 nm, which was consistent with the previous report [8]. And Fig. S1A, curve b suggested that the photoluminescent (PL) emission spectra of N-CQDs solution showed emission at 418 nm. According to the previous study, the CQDs showed emissions at 429 nm [26]. The 11 nm blue-shift of the PL emission was attributed to the strong electron affinity of N atoms doped in N-CQDs [27]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the N-CQDs in Fig. S1B showed a broad peak centering at around 23°, corresponding to an lattice spacing d of 0.38 nm, which was larger than that of graphite (0.34 nm). The increase of d value was attributed to the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups of the prepared N-CQDs [28]. Moreover, the d value of the N-CQDs was consistent with the lattice spacing of carbon-based materials with turbostratic disorder. The elemental analysis showed that the N-CQDs are composed of C, N and O elements at the percentages of 58.94%, 25.25% and 15.81%, respectively (See Supporting Information Fig. S5 and Table S1). This result confirmed that the N-CQDs have abundant oxygen-containing functional groups.

In order to achieve an optimal ECL signal, the pH of substrate solution was studied. As seen in Fig. 2 A, the ECL intensity of N-CQDs obtained a maximum value when the pH value of substrate solution was 7.5. What's more, the immobilized protein was damaged when it was in the situation of highly acidic or alkaline [29]. Therefore, the pH value of 7.5 was chosen as the optimal condition and used for testing throughout this study. As is shown in Fig. 2B, when the concentration of K2S2O8 in the substrate solution was 80 mM, N-CQDs had the maximum ECL emission.

In order to obtain a better understanding of the fabrication process of the ECL immunosensor, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was also carried out at different modified electrodes in 2.5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (Fig. 3 ). As is known that the impedance spectra consists of a semicircle portion and a linear portion, and the semicircle diameter at higher frequencies represents the electron-transfer resistance (Ret), and the linear part at lower frequencies represents the diffusion process. As is shown in Fig. 3, curve a showed the EIS of the bare GCE and the small semicircle attributed to a free electron-transfer process. Compared with bare GCE, the diameter of this semicircle increased remarkably when the N-CQDs was immobilized on the surface of GCE (carve b), which indicated that the N-CQDs hindered the electron transfer. When the Ab1 was conjugated on the N-CQDs modified electrode, a significant increase of the semicircle diameter was observed (curve c). Then curve d showed the Ret value increased, implying the GCE was successively blocked by BSA. Further binding of AFP retarded the interfacial electron transfer of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− pair, as the following in curve e. In the final step, an obvious increase of semicircle domain was observed again (curve f), demonstrating that the capture of NH2-G@Ab2 was successful.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL quenching mechanism between NH2-G and N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explain the quenching effect of NH2-G, the control experiments were conducted. All the experiment conditions are the same except for the labeling method of second antibody. The ECL intensity of N-CQDs modified electrode (GCE/N-CQDs) was 15000 (Fig. 4 A, curve a). When detecting the same concentration of AFP (20 ng/mL), the performances of the immunosensors were different. The ECL intensity of GCE/N-CQDs/Ab1/BSA/AFP/Ab2 was 6000 (Fig. 4A, curve b). As a comparison, the ECL intensity of GCE/N-CQDs/Ab1/BSA/AFP/NH2-G@Ab2 was 3000 (Fig. 4A, curve c). These phenomena could indicate that NH2-G could act as ECL quenching material of N-CQDs.

In order to further demonstrate the quenching mechanism between NH2-G and N-CQDs, more experiments were conducted. The ECL intensity of N-CQDs (10 mg/mL) which added into the substrate solution (PBS (pH = 7.5) containing 0.1 M KCl and 20 mM K2S2O8) was 9000 (Fig. 4B, curve a). Upon the addition of NH2-G to the N-CQDs containing solution, the ECL intensity of the N-CQDs decreased rapidly with the increased concentration of NH2-G and reached the highest ECL quenching efficiency of 99.4% at 3 mg/mL NH2-G (Fig. 4B, curve e), while the quenching efﬁciency of 57.1% was obtained for N-CQDs upon the addition of 0.5 mg/mL NH2-G (Fig. 4B, curve b). The decrease of the ECL intensity in the presence of NH2-G could be attributed to ERET from N-CQDs to NH2-G. S.Q. Liu et al. have demonstrated that the quenching of Ru1 by graphene oxide due to ERET [30].

To gain a better understanding of the quenching effect of NH2-G, the fluorescence intensity was performed at different concentrations of NH2-G in N-CQDs solution. Fig. S4 showed the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs (1 mg/mL) with the absence (curve a) and presence of different concentrations of NH2-G (curve b, c, d, e). With the addition of NH2-G to the N-CQDs containing solution, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs decreased rapidly with the increased concentration of NH2-G. Besides, GO has been proved to be a good energy acceptor that could quench the ﬂuorescence of organic dye through ﬂuorescence resonance energy transfer [31,32].

Operational stability was taken into consideration for the practical application of immunosensor. Fig. 6 A showed the ECL emission of GCE/N-CQDs/Ab1/BSA under 8 cycles of continuous potential scans between −2 and 0 V in PBS (pH = 7.5) containing 0.1 M KCl and 80 mM K2S2O8 at 100 mV/s. Strong and stable ECL signals were observed with the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.7%. The results suggested the excellent stability of the proposed immunosensor.

As shown in Fig. 6B, the specificity of the immunosensor was carried out under the optimal condition by using glucose, carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and cancer antigen (CA 15-3). 1 ng/mL of AFP solution containing 100 ng/mL of interfering substances was measured by the designed immunosensor. The RSD of the measurements was 0.43%. The result indicated that the selectivity of the immunosensor was acceptable.

To examine the reproducibility of the fabricated immunosensor, five electrodes were prepared for the detection of AFP. (Fig. 6C) RSD of measurements was 0.69%. The results indicated that the reproducibility of the immunosensor was good.

The analytical application of the ECL immunosensor was verified by detecting the recoveries different concentrations of AFP in human serum samples by standard addition methods (Table 2 ). The results demonstrated that the immunosensor could be used as a promising tool for clinical determination of AFP levels.

In order to further validate the proposed immunosensor, a comparison with the commercialized available enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) method is shown in Table S2. The relative error between the two methods was in the range from −1.9% to 2.6%. These data revealed a good agreement between the two analytical methods, further indicating the feasibility of the proposed immunosensor for clinical application.

In conclusion, an ECL immunoassay was developed by using N-CQDs as ECL basic material with the advantages of their non-toxicity, economy and stability. N-CQDs, as a luminophor, were successfully synthesized by carbonization of DTPA. The quenching mechanism between N-CQDs and NH2-G was firstly used to construct the immunosensor. The proposed immunosensor showed high sensitivity and wide linear, which will provide an attractive approach for other target determinations.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21175057, 21375047, 21377046), the Science and Technology Plan Project of Jinan (No. 201307010), the Science and Technology Development Plan of Shandong Province (No. 2014GSF120004), the Special Project for Independent Innovation and Achievements Transformation of Shandong Province (2014ZZCX05101), and QW thanks the Special Foundation for Taishan Scholar Professorship of Shandong Province and UJN (No. ts20130937).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2015.07.018.

Fabrication of GO/PANi/CdSe nanocomposites for sensitive electrochemiluminescence biosensor
Electrochemiluminescence of water-soluble carbon nanocrystals released electrochemically from graphite

Targeted retardation of hepatocarcinoma cells by specific replacement of alpha-fetoprotein RNA
AFP (alpha fetoprotein): who are you in gastrology?

Fast one-step synthesis of N-doped carbon dots by pyrolyzing ethanolamine

Electrochemiluminescent detection of mucin 1 protein and MCF-7 cancer cells based on the resonance energy transfer
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer between quantum dots and graphene oxide for sensing biomolecules
A graphene platform for sensing biomolecules

A label-free amperometric immunosensor for alpha-fetoprotein determination based on highly ordered porous multi-walled carbon nanotubes/silica nanoparticles array platform
Enzyme-free electrochemical immunosensor configured with Au-Pd nanocrystals and N-doped graphene sheets for sensitive detection of AFP
Sandwich-type electrochemiluminescence immunosensor based on Ru-silica@Au composite nanoparticles labeled anti-AFP
